system_prompt:
You are writing the "statement of outputs" — a human-readable narrative that
synthesizes everything the book teaches into a coherent description of the master/work's method.

The user will give you the book-level distillation, the chapter summaries, and
the structured systems-draft JSON you just derived. Write the statement.

Structure:

  # <Work Title> — Statement of Outputs

  ## Overview
  (2-3 paragraphs on what the book is and what it teaches, citing the
  book-level distillation.)

  ## Systems
  For each system in the systems-draft, one ## section. For each:
    - one paragraph describing the system in plain language
    - the system's members listed
    - the traversal rules and modification rules listed with their
      engine_payload kinds shown in code formatting
    - citations back to the relevant chapter summaries that backed each
      claim (e.g. "(Chapter 2 summary; ch02.md quotes)")

  ## Pending Work
  List every `_pending:<kebab>` engine_payload.kind value with what it
  signals. If there are none, omit this section.

  ## Provenance Notes
  Brief: what's drawn from what. Note any chapters that didn't yield a
  system and why.

This is the human-facing artifact. Be precise, cite, and don't invent
content outside the summaries and quote files.

# Book-level distillation — complete-course-vol-2

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

Mickey Baker's method is organized as a cumulative harmonic curriculum that moves from discrete voicing shapes toward integrated performance. Chapters 1 through 5 establish the vocabulary: Upper Partials as the governing harmonic concept, chord forms as the structural object of study, and the moving bass line and passing-tone voice leading as the first expressive tools. Chapters 6 through 11 shift from vocabulary to syntax, teaching the dominant-to-tonic cadence, chord cycle resolutions, relative-chord substitution, and forced harmony as the mechanisms by which chords become progressions. Chapters 12 through 19 introduce Symmetric Harmony and its associated devices—Symmetric Cycles, Augmented Cycles, Pedal Point, and the Cadence Break—as a parallel harmonic universe operating without a diatonic tonal center. Chapters 20 through 25 complete the edifice with chord analysis, diminished and augmented substitutions, and finally Counterpoint, which reframes everything previously learned as harmonic support for independent melodic lines. The final three chapters (27–29) contain no prose instruction; they are fully notated chord-melody solos by the author that function as comprehensive worked demonstrations of the entire method in performance context.

## Characteristic Systems and Terminology

Two master systems underlie the entire method. The first is **functional diatonic harmony** organized around the II–V–I resolution chain: the dominant (V) is the gravitational center of any tonality; the II chord prepares it; all elaborations—however many chords are inserted into a cycle—must eventually reach V and resolve to I (Chapter 9's governing rule). Chapter 8 establishes the I–IV–V–I and I–II–V–I progressions as memorization prerequisites before any substitution work begins. The second system is **Symmetric Harmony**, introduced in Chapter 13 as a key-signature-free framework in which every chord functions as a tonic and harmonic motion distributes equally across the 12-tone scale. Its practical notation is T1 through T4 (four equal divisions via minor thirds) or three divisions via major thirds for Augmented Cycles (Chapter 17), extending to six or twelve tonics by inserting intermediate chords. The diminished 7th chord is the emblematic vehicle of Symmetric Harmony: constructed from successive minor thirds, it maintains the same pitch classes across all inversions and positions, making every one of its tones a potential tonic. Chapter 26 explicitly names Symmetric Harmony and Counterpoint as the two principal theoretical systems the entire method rests on.

The load-bearing terminology, accumulated lesson by lesson, forms a precise lexicon: *Upper Partials* (the harmonic overtones that motivate modern voicing color); *chord forms* (voicing shapes distinct from rhythm chords); *moving bass line* (single-note melodic motion beneath static chords); *passing tones* (the voice-leading mechanism connecting ninth to sixth chords); *R* and *P* notation for Root and Passing tone in chord diagrams; *forced harmony* (adding chords via progressions that move away from and back to the tonic to keep music active); *Symmetric Cycle* (transposing any chord shape in minor thirds up or down the fingerboard); *Augmented Cycle* (transposing by major thirds); *Pedal Point* (sustaining a single tone—typically the fifth—while harmonies shift above it); *Cadence Break* (a one-to-two-bar structural pause at phrase endings); *Cantus Firmus* and *Counter melody* (the two independent voices of counterpoint); and *Rhythmic Chord and Melody Playing* (guitar as harmonic-melodic background in ensemble contexts).

## Rules and Prescriptions

The method establishes several explicit, actionable rules. When a voicing is physically awkward in its notated position, move it up the neck to a higher chord rather than force the fingering (Chapter 1). The fourth finger must be practiced chromatically across frets 1 through 10 to build the dexterity required for chord forms (Chapter 2). Beginners struggling with first-position five-fret stretches may begin in fourth or fifth position, with the expectation that facility arrives through practice rather than struggle (Chapter 4). All exercises must be analyzed thoroughly and then transposed chromatically up and down the entire fingerboard in both minor and major modes—this chromatic transposition drill is a standing practice rule, first stated in Chapter 5. Chapter 9 states the harmonic constraint governing all elaborations: no matter how many substitute or passing chords are added to a cycle, the progression must eventually reach the dominant and resolve to the tonic. Chapter 14 mandates retrograde practice—playing Symmetric Cycle exercises backwards from position 4 to position 1—to build descending facility. In chord-melody arranging, the melodic line takes priority over harmonic completeness: chords must be omitted or subordinated when they conflict with the melody (Chapter 26). Chapter 19 prescribes an absolute study method: exercises must be internalized through memorization and diligent repetition, never merely glanced at. Chapter 11 adds the complementary principle that after mastering all variations and committing them to memory, the student selects only those that align with personal musical taste—balancing technical command with artistic choice.

## Method and Methodology

The book's pedagogy is built on three interlocking methodological moves. The first is **dual-line contrast**: from Chapter 5 onward, standard progressions appear on one line and modern progressions on a second, allowing the student to hear the gap between traditional dominant-to-tonic resolution and the more colorful ninth-to-sixth voice leading the method teaches. The second is **cross-referenced voicing architecture**: circled numbers above chords refer back to the foundational voicing chart from Lesson 1, so every new exercise is explicitly anchored to previously learned material rather than introduced in isolation. The third is **systematic expansion of substitution privilege**: the method grants the student increasing license to deviate from written chord changes—Chapter 10 explicitly positions the player's ear and the "musical sounds that sound best to you" as the authority for substitutions, and asserts that jazz arrangements characteristically diverge from standard progressions—but this freedom is earned through the prior chapters' disciplined memorization of cycles, resolutions, and voicing forms.

The explicit performance methodology culminates in chord-melody playing (developed across Chapters 20–26), where arpeggiated bass voices carry independent melodic content, non-standard voicings are invented to fit the melodic line, and Counterpoint—two independent melodies moving simultaneously, one as Cantus Firmus and one as Counter melody—becomes the conceptual destination the entire harmonic study has been building toward. Ensemble application is handled in Chapters 23–24, where Pedal Point is distributed across players: the bass sustains the pedal tone, the guitar or piano executes harmonic variations above it, and a melody instrument carries the song's line. The three closing solos (Chapters 27–29) embody this synthesis, presenting Upper Partial voicings, symmetric cycles, and chord-melody writing as a unified performance language rather than separate technical modules.

# Chapter 1 — Chord Forms

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 introduces Upper Partials as the method's foundational concept for modern harmonic color in guitar voicings, establishing that students will study chord forms designed to exploit these overtones rather than traditional rhythm or comping chords. The chapter's scope is deliberately restricted: students learn harmonic vocabulary and voicing shapes while assuming a rhythmic foundation comes from other instruments, placing the focus entirely on upper-partial coloration and resonance. The master's characteristic move appears in the transposition advice—when a voicing proves difficult in its notated position, move it up the neck to a higher chord rather than forcing awkward fingering—a pragmatic principle that honors both the harmonic intent and the player's physical comfort. Load-bearing terminology here is twofold: Upper Partials names the harmonic resource the method explores, while chord forms (distinct from rhythm chords) identifies the specific object of study. Chapter 1 functions as pure vocabulary building; the movement and application of these forms—the actual harmonic narrative—begins in Lesson 2.

# Chapter 2 — Chord Forms cont'd

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 introduces Chord Forms, the method's foundational concept, which are structures that evolve by sustaining certain tones while moving others freely up or down the fretboard—a voice-leading mechanism demonstrated across four examples. The fourth finger plays a major role in forming these structures and must be practiced chromatically across frets 1 through 10 to develop the dexterity and precision required. A defining characteristic of these chord progressions is that the bottom tone of each chord moves down a whole tone, establishing the harmonic motion that drives the exercises. The chapter's pedagogical approach emphasizes systematic, chromatic practice to build muscle memory and internalize the relationship between finger position and chord voicing. This combination of sustained tones, mobile voices, and consistent bass motion creates the framework for understanding how chords can be transformed through position shifts rather than finger repositioning.

# Chapter 3 — Chord Exercises

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches chord exercises that bridge Modern and Standard guitar playing techniques through a series of structured drills. The core technical concept is the moving bass line—a single-note progression played underneath a static chord—which creates a counterpoint texture that may appear complex in notation but becomes straightforward once understood as independent melodic motion below harmonic anchors. By working through these exercises, players develop the ability to voice chords while maintaining a flowing bass line, a skill that distinguishes advanced playing from beginner technique. The chapter's characteristic move is the pedagogical patience of acknowledging notation strangeness upfront, explaining that the apparent complexity resolves once the moving line is heard as melodically separate from the chord. This lesson represents a deliberate closure of the technical gap between Modern and Standard approaches, making it foundational for balanced guitar facility. The load-bearing terms introduced are "moving bass line" and the implicit distinction between harmonic (chord) and melodic (bass) layers that coexist in the same passage.

# Chapter 4 — Simple Chord Analysis

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches simple chord analysis through position-aware exercises that move from minor ninth to minor sixth chords and major ninth to major sixth chords, all via passing tones, establishing harmonic motion as the conceptual framework for both minor and major modes. The master's characteristic move is pragmatic scaffolding: while exercises begin in first position requiring a full five-fret stretch, students who find this difficult are permitted to start in fourth or fifth position, acknowledging that eventual facility comes through practice rather than forced struggle. The chapter introduces essential load-bearing terminology—the chord pair types (minor ninth, minor sixth, major ninth, major sixth), the mechanism of resolution (passing tones), and the notational legend ("R" for Root, "P" for Passing tone) that governs how every chord diagram on the page must be read. By placing major and minor forms side by side and noting that mode is their sole structural difference, the chapter reinforces harmonic parallelism as a core organizing principle.

# Chapter 5 — Chords and Passing Tones

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 introduces the dual-line exercise format that becomes the method's signature teaching device, presenting standard chord progressions on the first line and modern progressions on the second to contrast traditional and contemporary approaches. The chapter teaches ninth-to-sixth voice leading as the conceptual advancement of modern harmony, replacing the simpler dominant-to-tonic resolutions found in standard progressions, with exercises 10 and 11 demonstrating the move from minor ninth to minor sixth and major ninth to major sixth. The master's characteristic move is the chromatic transposition drill—a standing practice rule requiring students to analyze examples thoroughly, then move them chromatically up and down the fingerboard in both minor and major modes to internalize the progressions across the full neck. The chapter establishes the notation convention of circled numbers above each chord, which reference the foundational chord voicing chart from Lesson One, creating a systematic link between new exercises and previously learned material. This architecture of parallel progressions, chromatic transposition, and cross-referenced voicing forms the pedagogical backbone for mastering voice-leading principles.

# Chapter 6 — Dominant to Tonic

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 teaches the dominant-to-tonic (V to I) cadence, the foundational harmonic resolution in jazz and traditional music, demonstrated here through the movement from Bb to Eb, with instruction to practice this progression chromatically across the fingerboard. The master's characteristic move is the direct analytical engagement with voicings—students must identify which chord tones each note represents and determine which tones are omitted, training both ear and harmonic comprehension simultaneously. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes V (the dominant) and I (the tonic) as the poles of cadential resolution, along with the concept of chord tone analysis as a systematic approach to understanding voicing construction. The chapter establishes that while multiple cadential closings exist, the voicings presented in Exercises 10, 11, and 12 provide a sufficient toolkit for most purposes, while signaling that additional coloristic variations remain to be explored. This progression-centered pedagogy reflects the method's focus on functional harmony and the student's development as both a player and harmonic analyst.

# Chapter 7 — Chords and Song Forms

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 teaches the application of four specific chords (1, 4, 15, and 17) to well-known standard tunes, establishing a methodical approach to harmonic practice within real song repertoire. The master's characteristic move is the voice-leading progression from major or minor 9th chords to their 6th chord resolutions, a structural rule that governs both exercises and defines the harmonic motion being drilled. Through this practice, students develop toward counterpoint—the musical technique of two melodic lines playing against one another—which emerges as the conceptual destination these chord exercises build toward. The chapter introduces a universal fingering rule for chord diagrams: the first finger always falls behind the fret that represents the position the chord occupies, as exemplified by the Dm69 chord in fifth position. The load-bearing terminology includes chord position, the 9th and 6th chord qualities, and counterpoint itself—all foundational concepts that integrate harmonic knowledge with ensemble thinking.

# Chapter 8 — Resolutions of Chord Cycles

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 teaches the foundational concept of chord cycle resolutions, establishing that the dominant V chord demands resolution into the tonic I chord, whose arrival satisfies the ear and completes harmonic closure. The master's characteristic move is to organize all harmonic motion hierarchically around the dominant as the most active chord in a tonality, showing how any intermediate chord in a cycle eventually gravitates toward V before resolving to I. The chapter introduces and prioritizes two essential cycles: the I-IV-V-I progression as a basic model, and the I-II-V-I cycle as the primary focus, framing the latter as preparation for the substitution techniques to follow. Key terminology includes the tension-and-release dynamic of V→I resolution, the concept of "temporary resolution" for intermediate chords, and the recognition of the dominant as the harmonic center of gravitational pull. The chapter emphasizes memorization of these cycles as a prerequisite for understanding the minor II substitution that lesson 9 will introduce, making this chapter the essential theoretical foundation for advanced harmonic substitution work.

# Chapter 9 — Related Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 teaches the concept of relative chords—chords that share identical tones or where one chord's tones form a subset of another's (such as C6 and Am7, or Dm as the upper three tones of G9)—and applies this principle to chord substitution within progressions. The core technique demonstrates how to insert the II chord before the V chord in a I-V-I progression, transforming a simple four-bar skeleton (one bar I, two bars V, one bar I) into four elaborated variants that add intermediate chords while preserving the underlying harmonic motion. The master's characteristic move is methodical expansion: each of the four examples in Example 22 adds more chords to the cycle, yet all maintain the fundamental I-V-I shape, showing how substitution creates complexity without obscuring underlying structure. The load-bearing rule states that no matter how many chords are added to elaborate a cycle, they must eventually reach the dominant and resolve into the tonic, establishing the constraint that governs all permissible elaborations. This chapter introduces the concept of relative chords and the II-V substitution as foundational tools for reharmonizing simple progressions.

# Chapter 10 — Altered Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 introduces Forced Harmony—a compositional technique that adds chords via progressions moving away from and back to the tonic, keeping music active rather than static—and establishes the Upper Partials as the load-bearing concept for chord alteration: the dominant can be extended to 7th, 9th, 11th, augmented 11th, or 13th, with the 5th or 9th potentially flatted, all resolving to the tonic; the II chord follows the same principle with its own Upper Partials (6th, 7th, 9th, 11th, 13th) resolving through the V to the tonic, forming the jazz II-V-I resolution chain. The master's characteristic move is privileging player judgment and arranging creativity over literal adherence to original chord progressions, positioning the student's ear and "musical sounds that sound best to you" as the authority for substitutions and alterations. The chapter asserts that II can substitute for V and that jazz arrangements characteristically diverge from standard written progressions, granting the soloist explicit license to rebuild harmony through extended chords and harmonic movement rather than playing changes as written.

# Chapter 11 — Chords and Melody Playing

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Chapter 11 teaches the forced chord cycles method, which substitutes alternative chord progressions against the original cycles in song fragments to expand harmonic vocabulary and creative options. Students are presented with original song excerpts in sheet music format alongside two variations for each example, drawn from lesson 7 and additional songs. The load-bearing concept is the forced chord cycle itself—a deliberate harmonic substitution technique that allows players to reharmonize melodies while maintaining their melodic integrity. The chapter's characteristic pedagogical move is the two-variation format, which systematically presents alternatives and trains ear recognition of harmonic color. The practice directive is explicit and direct: master all variations, commit them to memory, then select and use only those that align with personal musical taste, balancing technical mastery with artistic choice.

# Chapter 12 — The Cadence

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch12.md`._

Chapter 12 teaches the Authentic Cadence, the harmonic gesture that ends a musical thought by moving from the dominant (V) chord to the tonic (I), establishing closure and tonality. The chapter anchors this concept in popular music's structural foundation of eight- or sixteen-bar phrases, where cadences mark formal boundaries. A characteristic distinction the master draws is between introductions and endings: introductions stop on the V chord before approaching the song's first chord, while endings complete the V-to-I motion as a final statement. The load-bearing terminology is "Authentic Cadence" itself, which names this V-I progression and its functional role in harmonic syntax. The master emphasizes that cadential thinking applies at any structural scale—not only in standard phrase lengths but in single- or two-bar sequences—making the Authentic Cadence a scalable principle rather than a fixed form.

# Chapter 13 — Symetric Harmony

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch13.md`._

Symmetric Harmony is a key-signature-free system in which chords progress freely without the hierarchical tonal center of diatonic harmony, introduced at cadences and structural breaks where the tonic chord anchors endings. The chapter's core principle states that in Symmetric Harmony every chord functions as a tonic, with practitioners selecting any number of tonics; the practical framework divides the 12-tone scale into four equal parts using a T1-through-T4 notation system where each chord is labeled with a "T" and number to indicate which tonic it represents. The master technique centers on the diminished 7th chord, which moves freely up and down the fingerboard in minor thirds, maintaining the same tones across positions but in different arrangements. Because a diminished chord is constructed from successive minor thirds, each tone within it serves as a potential root or tonic, making the chord enharmonically equivalent regardless of inversion or position—a property demonstrated in Exercise 31b. This system exemplifies the characteristic move of treating harmonic movement as a non-linear, symmetrically distributed space rather than a tonally centered journey.

# Chapter 14 — Symetric Cycles

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch14.md`._

Chapter 14 introduces Symmetric Cycles, a technique for transposing chord shapes by moving them up or down the fretboard in ascending or descending minor thirds, with only the first chord diagrammed since the remaining positions follow the same shape at the higher intervals. The chapter presents four written examples and establishes a characteristic left-hand move where the fourth finger transfers from the 2nd to the 3rd string as the player progresses up the fretboard in these minor-third increments. The master emphasizes that any chord can be played as a Symmetric Cycle, whether ascending from a low position or descending from a high position, making this a universal transposition tool rather than a context-specific technique. A practical constraint acknowledges that cutaway guitars allow extension to higher positions while non-cutaway instruments are limited by their body design. The chapter includes a retrograde practice rule directing students to play the exercises backwards (from position 4 back to 1) to build facility with descending motion, cementing "Symmetric Cycle" and "minor third" as the load-bearing terminology for this transposition vocabulary.

# Chapter 15 — Symetric Introductions and Endings — Part 1

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch15.md`._

Chapter 15 teaches how to apply the Symmetric Cycle—a harmonic device that transcends diatonic harmony—as an ending device to create dynamic, unexpected closures in guitar compositions. The master's characteristic move is the directional glissando in minor thirds: if a phrase ends high on the fingerboard, move the final chord down in minor thirds; if it ends low, move it up. The chapter introduces a concrete application method: take existing introductions and endings from earlier lessons and replace their final chords with Symmetric Cycle endings, as demonstrated in Exercises 36 and 37, which mirror the opening bars of Exercises 29C and 30A before concluding with this technique. A key expansion is that Symmetric Cycle need not use the entire chord—partial chord fragments (as shown in Lesson 11, Exercises 25A and 25B) can execute the same glissando movement, creating smoother voice-leading while maintaining the harmonic surprise. The load-bearing terminology centers on the Symmetric Cycle itself, minor thirds as the intervallic vehicle for the motion, and the concept of chord fragments as viable alternatives to complete voicings.

# Chapter 16 — Symetric Introductions and Endings — Part 2

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch16.md`._

Chapter 16 expands the symmetric-cycle technique beyond linear minor-third motion, demonstrating that these patterns can be applied in varied harmonic contexts to create introductions and endings. The master's characteristic move is retrograde application—taking previously learned broken-chord exercises and reversing them to generate new material, as shown in Example 34's inversion of Example 33. The load-bearing terminology centers on "Symmetric Cycles" and "broken chords played backwards," which enable guitarists to construct mirror-image phrases that feel cohesive and musical. The chapter's core directive is to internalize these variations through practice and apply them to songs the student already knows, embedding the technique into real musical contexts rather than treating it as abstract theory.

# Chapter 17 — Augmented Cycles

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch17.md`._

Chapter 17 teaches augmented chord construction based on major thirds and their symmetric properties across the chromatic scale. An augmented chord (such as C–E–G#) maintains its pitch-class identity when transposed up a major third, enabling the formation of an "Augmented Cycle" by dividing the 12-tone scale into three equal parts to yield three distinct tonic chords, a principle demonstrated in Exercises 38–40. The chapter expands this framework to accommodate six tonics by inserting intermediate chords into the cycle (Exercise 41), and extends further to introduce "Symmetric Harmony," a technique in which parallel voice leading connects start and end harmonic points regardless of intervening motion, permitting up to 12 tonics within a single progression (Exercise 42). The master's characteristic approach treats harmonic motion as a matter of endpoints and direction rather than voice-leading detail, emphasizing the mathematical economy of equal division and transposition as organizing principles for harmonic exploration.

# Chapter 18 — Augmented 7ths

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch18.md`._

Chapter 18 introduces the Augmented 7th chord, formed by adding a 7th interval to the Augmented triad, as demonstrated in Exercise 43. This chapter establishes a fundamental principle governing non-diatonic harmony: chords that do not appear in a song's original chord structure can be strategically introduced to add color and originality to the arrangement. The Augmented 7th represents one of several substitute chord options available to the guitarist seeking harmonic variety beyond the diatonic palette. The key terminology this chapter loads is "Augmented 7th" and the concept of non-diatonic color chords as deliberate harmonic choices rather than errors. By understanding this relationship between chord extension and tonal color, players learn to expand their harmonic vocabulary beyond the constraints of traditional songwriting structure.

# Chapter 19 — Application of Symetric Cycles

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch19.md`._

Rhythmic Chord and Melody Playing is a technique where the guitar provides harmonic and melodic background accompaniment rather than leading the melody, making it ideal for small combo settings with bass and drums where the guitar can replace the traditional role of piano. The chapter defines this approach as distinct from lead playing, focusing on fill-ins and fragments that support an external melody source such as a vocalist or solo instrument. A master's characteristic move evident here is the pragmatic ensemble philosophy—demonstrating how mastery of this technique enables leaner, more efficient group compositions without sacrificing harmonic richness. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes "Rhythmic Chord and Melody Playing" itself, which designates a specific functional role, and the concept of "background" playing as a deliberate positioning choice rather than accompaniment by default. The chapter's pedagogy emphasizes an absolute study method: students must practice the following exercises until internalized through memorization and repeated, diligent review, establishing that superficial glancing is insufficient and deep integration through repetition is mandatory.

# Chapter 20 — Chord Analysis

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch20.md`._

Chapter 20 teaches chord analysis through the critical insight that chords possess multiple names and enharmonic equivalences, requiring players to recognize the same chord structure bearing different names across different contexts. The master's characteristic move is prioritizing the bass voice in arpeggiated chord passages—allowing the lowest note of each chord to form an independent melodic line or counter-melody, which he demonstrates in Exercise 49 by dropping the original chord structure in favor of arpeggio patterns where the bass note drives originality. A foundational technique introduced here is chord-melody playing, where the arpeggiated bass creates harmonic foundation while carrying melodic content. The chapter establishes the load-bearing principle that chords need not follow "standard" fingerings when serving the melody: non-standard voicings are legitimate tools for musical blending and effect, requiring players to invent chords that fit both the melodic line and the harmonic cycle being created. This adaptive approach—creating chords for specific musical context rather than relying only on familiar forms—becomes essential as harmonic complexity increases.

# Chapter 21 — Diminished and Augmented Resolutions

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch21.md`._

Chapter 21 teaches that diminished 7th chords and augmented chords function as substitutes for dominant chords in V-I resolutions, with the diminished 7th reframed not as a chord in itself but as the upper structure tones of a flattened 9th dominant chord. The master demonstrates that a root-omitted G7b9 voicing is harmonically equivalent to a B diminished chord, establishing a practical equivalence between the two sonorities. The characteristic move illustrated throughout is the direct resolution of either diminished or augmented chords into the tonic, which the chapter explicitly identifies as a V-I resolution equivalent despite the absence of a traditional dominant. This principle extends the family of "altered chords" introduced earlier in the method, allowing students to substitute these colorful voicings wherever a dominant chord would normally resolve to tonic. The load-bearing terminology here centers on the concept of "upper tones" of a flat-9 chord structure and the principle of harmonic substitution, which permit these non-diatonic sonorities to function as tension-and-release devices within the tonal framework.

# Chapter 22 — The Cadence Break

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch22.md`._

The Cadence Break is a structural pause occurring at the end of musical thoughts where the music relaxes for one to two bars before repeating or progressing to new material, and it can be filled with Symmetric Cycles, Pedal Point, or original chord progressions to add color and originality. Pedal Point, the chapter's cornerstone technique, sustains a single note—typically the fifth of the scale—across multiple measures while varying chords play overhead, with the most popular form in C Major using a G pedal supporting Dm9 and G13b9 chords. The master demonstrates characteristic resourcefulness by showing how to adapt Pedal Point across keys through strategic retuning: lowering the 6th string a whole tone to D for G Major, lowering the 5th string a whole tone to G for C Major in half-note rhythm, and raising the 5th string a half tone to Bb for Eb Major. The chapter introduces flexibility in rhythm by specifying that the pedal note can be articulated as whole notes, half notes, quarter notes, or eighth notes, giving players multiple interpretive choices within the same harmonic framework. Load-bearing terms include "Cadence Break" (the structural pause), "Pedal Point" (the sustained-note technique), and "half-note Pedal Point" (a rhythmic variant), which together form the vocabulary for understanding how to create introductions and transitions with harmonic sophistication.

# Chapter 23 — Pedal Point

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch23.md`._

Chapter 23 introduces the pedal point technique, a foundational harmonic device that sustains a single note (typically the root or fifth of a key) while harmonies move above it, creating tension and harmonic interest. Students learn to identify and execute pedal point on D in G major, where D functions as the fifth degree and serves as the fixed anchor while other chord voices shift independently. The chapter uses drop-D tuning on the sixth string as the prerequisite setup, enabling the sustained D to resonate naturally throughout the exercises. By isolating pedal point as the single variable added to an existing jazz guitar exercise, the pedagogy makes explicit what students are learning to control—the deliberate contrast between harmonic movement and tonal stability. The load-bearing terminology centers on pedal point itself, harmonic function (the V, or fifth degree), and the relationship between sustained tones and changing harmonies above them.

# Chapter 24 — Practical Use of Pedal Point

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch24.md`._

Chapter 24 teaches the practical orchestration of Pedal Point in ensemble settings, establishing that the bass player holds the sustained pedal tone while the piano or guitar provides harmonic variations above it. The master's characteristic move is the three-layer texture—bass playing the pedal point, guitar or piano executing chord variations, and a melody or solo instrument carrying the song's line—which defines the method's functional model for this technique. The chapter introduces the distinction between pedagogical exercises (Exercises 57 and 59, designed for ear-training only) and performance-ready arrangements (Exercises 60 and 61), clarifying which patterns transfer to live playing. Load-bearing terminology includes "Pedal Point distribution" to describe how the sustained note must be allocated based on ensemble size, and "Cadence Breaks," identifying structural moments where Pedal Point appropriately extends beyond its primary harmonic function. This chapter transitions Pedal Point from isolated technical study into the practical domain of ensemble arrangement and performance contexts.

# Chapter 25 — Counterpoint

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch25.md`._

Counterpoint is the movement of two or more independent melodies simultaneously rather than the use of chord harmony, representing an essential technique in modern music that derives from classical tradition and particularly benefits small ensemble playing. The fundamental rule is that when two lines move against one another, one melody can sustain itself for several beats while the other line moves in faster note values, creating rhythmic contrast and textural variety. The technical terminology labels these voices as the Cantus Firmus (the original melody) and the Counter melody (the added melody), though simpler names suffice in practice. The master's characteristic approach prioritizes harmonic integrity above all—the examples avoid bad harmonic movement and move chordally from one direction to another without lingering excessively on single notes, ensuring that melodic independence never compromises the underlying harmonic foundation. A key technique is the sustaining counter melody, which extracts only the first note from each chord to create a strong, straight-line bass quality while the primary melody dances freely above it, as demonstrated in the progression from Exercise 49 to Exercise 65.

# Chapter 26 — Summary

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch26.md`._

Chapter 26 teaches that exercises in chord-melody arranging must prioritize the melodic line above harmonic completeness, requiring students to omit or subordinate chords when they conflict with the melody—a foundational principle demonstrated through scored examples marked with cross-references to earlier techniques like the Cadence Break and Symmetric cycle. The chapter positions Symmetric Harmony and Counterpoint as the two principal theoretical systems underlying the entire method, signaling that students should deepen their study of these subjects beyond the introductory treatment provided. The master's characteristic move is visible in the careful annotation of the score itself, using footnote markers to direct the student's attention to specific moments where chords are "forced against the melody" or deliberately dropped, embedding the teaching directly into the musical examples rather than abstract rules. Key terminology includes the Symmetric cycle (a specific harmonic technique applied at marked points) and the Cadence Break (a structural device for phrase termination), both invoked by the exercises to show how theory translates to actual performance and arranging decisions. Through this chapter's exercises, students learn that harmonic knowledge serves melody—not the reverse—and are encouraged to view this method as a gateway to deeper harmonic study rather than a comprehensive treatment.

          "summary": "If a phrase ends high on the fingerboard move the cycle downward in minor thirds; if it ends low, move it upward.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "StringSetTransition"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 15,
              "topic": "Applying Symmetric Cycle to existing material",
              "quote_excerpt": "If the last chord ends high on the finger board, move it down in minor thirds, if it ends low, move it up.",
              "page": 24
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "retrograde-cycle-practice",
          "name": "Retrograde Cycle Traversal",
          "summary": "Symmetric Cycle exercises are practiced backwards (T4-T3-T2-T1) to build descending facility.",

          },
          "references": [

        {
          "id": "parallel-motion-endpoint-rule",
          "name": "Parallel Motion / Endpoint-Only Rule",

          "references": [

    {
      "id": "mickey-baker:complete-course-vol-2:counterpoint-chord-melody",
      "name": "Counterpoint and Chord-Melody System",
      "summary": "The culminating system, framing prior learning as harmonic support for independent melodic lines. Counterpoint is the movement of two or more independent melodies \u2014 Cantus Firmus and Counter melody \u2014 rather than chord harmony. Members include the two melodic voices, arpeggiated bass voice, and Pedal Point. Traversal rules govern rhythmic contrast between voices and the requirement that chordal motion avoids bad harmonic movement. Modification rules govern arpeggiated bass extraction as counter-melody, Pedal Point distribution across ensemble, and chord omission for melody priority.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "cantus-firmus",
          "name": "Cantus Firmus (Original Melody)",
          "summary": "Primary melodic voice against which the counter melody moves."
        },
        {
          "id": "counter-melody",
          "name": "Counter Melody (Added Voice)",
          "summary": "Second independent melodic line; may be extracted as the first note of each arpeggiated chord."
        },
        {
          "id": "arpeggiated-bass-voice",
          "name": "Arpeggiated Bass Voice",
          "summary": "Lowest note of each arpeggiated chord, treated as independent melodic line."
        },
        {
          "id": "pedal-point",
          "name": "Pedal Point",
          "summary": "Sustained single pitch (canonically the fifth of the scale) held while harmonies shift above it."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "rhythmic-contrast-between-voices",
          "name": "Rhythmic Contrast Between Voices",
          "summary": "One voice sustains (up to four beats) while the other moves in quarter or eighth notes.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "_pending:voice-rhythmic-contrast"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 25,
              "topic": "Two-line movement rule",
              "quote_excerpt": "one melody sustains itself for as many as 4 beats, while the other line moves along in quarter or eighth notes.",
              "page": 41
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "harmonic-integrity-constraint",
          "name": "Harmonic Integrity Constraint",
          "summary": "Melodic independence must never compromise the harmonic foundation; examples avoid bad harmonic movement.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "FamilyCoherence"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 25,
              "topic": "Harmonic movement standard",
              "quote_excerpt": "there is never a sign or sound of bad harmonic movement.",
              "page": 41
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "pedal-point-ensemble-distribution",
          "name": "Pedal Point Ensemble Distribution",
          "summary": "Bass plays the pedal tone; guitar or piano executes chord variations above; a melody instrument carries the song's line.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "_pending:ensemble-layer-assignment"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 24,
              "topic": "Pedal Point distribution by ensemble",
              "quote_excerpt": "the bass plays the Pedal Point while the piano or guitar plays the chord variations.",
              "page": 40
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 24,
              "topic": "Three-voice arrangement with Pedal Point",
              "quote_excerpt": "bass playing Pedal Point, the guitar playing the chord variations and the voice or solo instrument carrying the melody",
              "page": 40
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [
        {
          "id": "arpeggiated-bass-counter-melody-extraction",
          "name": "Arpeggiated Bass Counter-Melody Extraction",
          "summary": "Only the first (lowest) note of each arpeggiated chord is retained to form a sustaining counter-melody line.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "VoiceMotion"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 25,
              "topic": "Sustaining counter melody technique",
              "quote_excerpt": "only the first note in each chord of the counter melody is used. This is a sustaining type of counter melody",
              "page": 41
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 20,
              "topic": "arpeggiated bass melodic line",
              "quote_excerpt": "the first note of each chord is the most important. It forms a melodic line at the base of the chord",
              "page": 33
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "pedal-point-fifth-canonical",
          "name": "Pedal Point Fifth-of-Scale Canonical Choice",
          "summary": "The fifth of the scale is the canonical sustained pedal tone.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "ColorToneRequire"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 22,
              "topic": "Pedal Point definition",
              "quote_excerpt": "The most popular note in Pedal Point is the fifth of the scale.",
              "page": 37
            },

              "topic": "Pedal point on D defined",
              "quote_excerpt": "Pedal Point on D, the V of Gmaj.",
              "page": 38
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "chord-omission-for-melody",
          "name": "Chord Omission for Melody Priority",
          "summary": "In chord-melody arranging, chords conflicting with the melodic line must be dropped.",
          "engine_payload": {

          "references": [
